title: First Startup Hires Fit

introduction.subtitle: Readiness assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
First Startup Hires Fit helps you assess Ambiguity Ownership, Learning Pace, Customer & Product Proximity, Team Values & Resilience through concrete operating questions. Answer from observable behaviour and current evidence, not aspiration.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How does the candidate respond when the role is only partly defined?

options.text: A. They wait for a precise job description before acting.
B. They can start, but repeatedly ask founders to resolve routine ambiguity.
C. They translate a vague role into clear next outcomes and confirm the riskiest assumptions.
D. They create useful ownership boundaries, act without over-escalating, and keep founders informed of trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Ambiguity Ownership: How does the candidate respond when the role is only partly defined
conceptHelp.summary: Ambiguity Ownership is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How does the candidate respond when the role is only partly defined?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Ambiguity Ownership matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questions.text: How naturally do they turn messy work into owned outcomes?

options.text: A. They prefer clean tasks and avoid messy cross-functional work.
B. They help when asked, but ownership fades when the work is unclear or unrewarded.
C. They turn messy work into a named outcome, plan, and visible owner.
D. They repeatedly take undefined work from mess to measurable progress without hoarding control.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questions.text: How do they behave when the founder is unavailable for a decision?

options.text: A. They stop or wait when the founder is unavailable.
B. They make small moves, but avoid decisions that require judgement.
C. They use principles, current goals, and reversibility to make a defensible call.
D. They make the call, document the reasoning, and flag what would change it.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How quickly do they learn a domain they have not worked in before?

options.text: A. They rely mainly on prior experience and struggle when the domain is new.
B. They learn basic terms, but need heavy guidance to identify what matters.
C. They build a learning map, ask useful questions, and test understanding quickly.
D. They compress learning through customers, data, experts, and fast written synthesis.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do they use feedback when the first version is wrong?

options.text: A. They defend the first version or wait for someone else to fix direction.
B. They accept feedback, but changes are slow or surface-level.
C. They update the work, explain what changed, and preserve useful parts.
D. They actively seek disconfirming feedback and turn it into a better second version fast.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How well do they separate useful speed from careless rushing?

options.text: A. They move fast by skipping context, quality checks, or alignment.
B. They can move quickly, but speed creates rework or confusion.
C. They choose what can be fast and what needs care, then communicate the trade-off.
D. They create speed through smaller scope, tight feedback loops, and clear risk controls.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How willing are they to speak directly with customers or users?

options.text: A. They avoid direct customer/user contact unless the role formally requires it.
B. They will join calls, but rarely seek customer evidence themselves.
C. They proactively speak with customers or users to improve their work.
D. They use customer contact as a normal operating input and bring back decision-ready insight.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How strongly do they connect their work to product and revenue learning?

options.text: A. They treat their work as output, separate from product or revenue learning.
B. They understand the link in theory, but do not use it to prioritise.
C. They can explain how their work affects product learning, conversion, retention, or revenue.
D. They choose and adapt work based on the fastest path to product and revenue learning.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do they handle evidence that customers do not care about their output?

options.text: A. They blame customers, sales, or timing when customers do not care.
B. They accept the evidence, but struggle to change the work materially.
C. They ask what was learned and adjust scope, segment, message, or quality.
D. They quickly separate ego from evidence and turn indifference into a sharper next test.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do they behave when priorities change abruptly?

options.text: A. They become passive, frustrated, or chaotic when priorities change.
B. They adapt after direction is repeated, but lose momentum or trust.
C. They re-plan work, clarify trade-offs, and keep commitments visible.
D. They help the team absorb change without drama and preserve momentum on what still matters.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do they handle credit, blame, and public disagreement?

options.text: A. They seek credit, avoid blame, or escalate disagreement politically.
B. They stay polite, but hard issues remain unresolved.
C. They share credit, own mistakes, and disagree with concrete reasons.
D. They make disagreement safer by focusing on evidence, outcome, and repair.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do they protect team trust while moving fast?

options.text: A. They move fast by bypassing people, context, or agreements.
B. They try to protect trust, but speed still creates avoidable surprises.
C. They communicate early, expose risks, and keep promises small enough to keep.
D. They build trust through fast loops, explicit boundaries, and reliable follow-through.
Not sure / not applicable.

questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over ideal self-description.
